summary: McKenzie reviews 'Blog Blazers,' a book he contributed a chapter to, which collects interviews with 40 successful bloggers including Seth Godin and Jeff Atwood. He highlights the fascinating diversity of answers to identical questions about blogging success, and notes areas where the authors disagree with each other. His own chapter focuses on practical blogging tips for small businesses, and he playfully acknowledges disagreeing with his marketing idol Seth Godin on the value of list posts. He recommends the book as a valuable resource for small software businesses trying to figure out how blogging fits into their marketing strategy.
key_points: The book's strength lies in the diversity of perspectives from 40 bloggers answering identical questions, revealing genuine disagreements about monetization, post length, SEO, and writing style.

McKenzie disagrees with Seth Godin on list posts, arguing they turn a blog into a commodity provider while acknowledging the underlying principles of promising benefits in titles and using formatting to engage readers.
His core blogging advice is to build relationships with two groups: aspirational peers who've achieved your goals, and near-peers who are slightly ahead of you on the path to success.
He recommends buying through Amazon rather than direct because publishing is a winners-win game where higher Amazon rank creates a virtuous cycle of visibility.
He humorously acknowledges not following his own advice about sustainable blogging pace, a recurring self-aware theme in his writing.
money_quotes: Recognize that you will be blogging until you stop blogging. That sounds simple, but many people start out with a burst of post-every-day fever, which they cannot sustain over the long haul. Pick a pace which is predictable and sustainable.
I personally can't stand the 'Top Ten Ways To Write A Blog Post' type articles, as aside from being boring and aesthetically unpleasant they turn your blog into a commodity provider of lists.
Titles which promise immediate benefits are a good idea and judicious use of formatting such as bullet points, bold text, and pictures can turn a scanning surfer into an engaged, active reader.

The folks who have achieved something close to what you want to achieve give you examples to emulate and objectives to strive for, and the folks just a wee bit farther than you should become your new best friends.
Seth Godin is one of the world's best living theorists about marketing. I am not Seth Godin. You are not Seth Godin. Please, for all that is holy, don't write list posts.
Publishing is a winners-win game, and purchases through Amazon increase the book's Amazon rank, which results in more prominent placement and indirect marketing opportunities.
key_insight: The best blogging advice comes from studying both aspirational successes and near-peers, and the real skill is extracting principles from common tactics rather than blindly copying formats like list posts.
